_provenance.context_preview:

Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Marine and Petroleum Geology journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/marpetgeo Research paper Mass transport deposit (MTD) relief as a control on post-MTD sedimentation: Insights from the Taranaki Basin, oﬀshore New Zealand Jeﬀerson Nwoko∗, Ian Kane, Mads Huuse Basin Studies, School of Natural Sciences, University of Manchester, UK A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Mass transport deposits (MTDs) Turbidite Stratigraphic trap Seismic facies A B S T R A C T Mass transport deposits (MTDs) can aﬀect the routing of turbidite systems and their resultant sedimentary ar- chitecture. With the aid of a high-quality 3D-seismic reﬂection dataset a buried Pleistocene MTD has been identiﬁed in the deep-water Taranaki Basin. Three MTD seismic facies have been identiﬁed and fall within three morphological domains – extensional, translational and contractional areas. Thickness relationships between the MTD and the overburden deposits were investigated quantitatively and matched using TWT-thickness maps. The results reveal that in proximal and distal areas with signiﬁcant MTD relief, occupied by megaclasts and folded facies respectively, post-MTD accommodation is dominated by small ‘micro-basins’ with tortuous sediment pathways. In contrast the translational domain, containing homogeneous MTD facies, hosts larger low-relief basins. These characteristics can be used to deﬁne two main types of depositional topography: Type I, High-reli

_provenance.query: Describe the seismic character of mass transport deposit including amplitude, continuity, and internal reflections.
_provenance.context_preview:
[CHUNK 1 | unknown]
The initial 2D analysis was further refined through the integration of geomorpho- logical elements. Seismic geomorphology techniques (Posamentier and Morris, 2000; Posamentier, 2004; Posamentier et al., 2007) were applied to identify key geological patterns at the map scale, which were subsequently examined in greater detail using the available seismic lines. The characterization of the MTDs was based on a detailed seismic facies analysis, which revealed lithofacies variations within the Upper Quaternary sedimentary unit. These deposits, often associated with gravity-driven downslope processes, were identified by their charac- teristic opaque, chaotic, and discontinuous seismic facies. MTDs, commonly referred to as submarine landslides, occur on submarine slopes and typically involve the downslope movement of materials, sometimes over considerable distances and volumes, into low-gradient areas (Hampton et al., 1996; Owen et al., 2007). Due to their high mobility, MTDs exhibit significant variability in seismic signatures (Mohrig et al., 1998; Gee et al., 1999). To characterize the MTDs, we first analyzed the geomorphological features and internal architecture, following the framework established by Moscardelli and Wood (2008). Seismic reflection data were inter- preted to identify variations in the continuity, amplitude, and configu- ration of the internal reflections. These parameters allowed us the recognition of gravitational deposits and their stratigr

_verification.reasoning: The source text directly states that the deposits have a "chaotic" seismic facies, which aligns with the claim.

The seismic facies associated with these MTD domains are described based on their seismic reﬂection attributes, amplitude re- ﬂection strength, internal conﬁguration, and continuity of the reﬂec- tions. MF description: This facies is well imaged and covers an area of approximately 413.82 km2. From northeast to southwest, a dominantly moderate to high amplitude reﬂection pattern that typically delineates angular protruding megaclasts is observed (Fig. 4b and c). The mega- clasts, measuring about 0.4–1.5 km high and 0.7 km–1 km in width, are internally stratiﬁed and characterised by coherent reﬂections that are separated by extensional planar contacts, encased within a chaotic mix (Fig. 4b and c). Within the same facies, a characteristic set of moder- ately deformed seismic reﬂections with some localised faults abruptly terminates against a series of relatively undeformed parallel reﬂectors (Fig. 4d). These undeformed parallel reﬂectors are mainly composed of symmetrical fore- and back-thrust sediment blocks (Fig. 4d). MF interpretation: This facies is interpreted to represent discrete megaclasts carried within a deformed (debrites) laminar ﬂow (Fig. 4b and c) (Frey-Martinez et al., 2005; Bull et al., 2009; Ortiz-Karpf et al., 2015; Omeru and Cartwright, 2019; Nwoko et al., 2020). The initial 2D analysis was further refined through the integration of geomorpho- logical elements. Seismic geomorphology techniques (Posamentier and Morris, 2000; Posamentier, 2004; Posamentier et al., 2007) were applied to identify key geological patterns at the map scale, which were subsequently examined in greater detail using the available seismic lines. The characterization of the MTDs was based on a detailed seismic facies analysis, which revealed lithofacies variations within the Upper Quaternary sedimentary unit. These deposits, often associated with gravity-driven downslope processes, were identified by their charac- teristic opaque, chaotic, and discontinuous seismic facies. MTDs, commonly referred to as submarine landslides, occur on submarine slopes and typically involve the downslope movement of materials, sometimes over considerable distances and volumes, into low-gradient areas (Hampton et al., 1996; Owen et al., 2007). Due to their high mobility, MTDs exhibit significant variability in seismic signatures (Mohrig et al., 1998; Gee et al., 1999). To characterize the MTDs, we first analyzed the geomorphological features and internal architecture, following the framework established by Moscardelli and Wood (2008). Seismic reflection data were inter- preted to identify variations in the continuity, amplitude, and configu- ration of the internal reflections. The initial 2D analysis was further refined through the integration of geomorpho- logical elements. Seismic geomorphology techniques (Posamentier and Morris, 2000; Posamentier, 2004; Posamentier et al., 2007) were applied to identify key geological patterns at the map scale, which were subsequently examined in greater detail using the available seismic lines. The characterization of the MTDs was based on a detailed seismic facies analysis, which revealed lithofacies variations within the Upper Quaternary sedimentary unit. These deposits, often associated with gravity-driven downslope processes, were identified by their charac- teristic opaque, chaotic, and discontinuous seismic facies. MTDs, commonly referred to as submarine landslides, occur on submarine slopes and typically involve the downslope movement of materials, sometimes over considerable distances and volumes, into low-gradient areas (Hampton et al., 1996; Owen et al., 2007). Due to their high mobility, MTDs exhibit significant variability in seismic signatures (Mohrig et al., 1998; Gee et al., 1999). To characterize the MTDs, we first analyzed the geomorphological features and internal architecture, following the framework established by Moscardelli and Wood (2008). Seismic reflection data were inter- preted to identify variations in the continuity, amplitude, and configu- ration of the internal reflections. Thus the healed topography in this seismically homogenous region (HF facies) leads to what we suggest to be a broad down dip ﬁll (Fig. 6a and c). This type of inﬁlling occurs where there is a large se- paration distance between topographic highs, in the order of hundreds of meters (MTD type II topography). In places where depositional rates exceed the rate of accommodation space formation, these sediment ponds become connected by aggrading sediments, ﬁlled to the local position of the equilibrium proﬁle (Fig. 6c). Therefore, overburden sediment connectivity in this region is consistent with the low-relief serrated proﬁle peculiar to laterally connected sediment distribution patterns (Kneller et al., 2016; Bull et al., 2020) and is shown for the HF facies with smooth upper surface topography (Fig. 5c, d, 5e and 6a). 6. is found within the sedimentary sequence. This unit is characterized by a transparent and chaotic seismic facies (Fig. 8). 4.2. Other recent mass movement systems On the northeast flank of Banghittu High, the here called Banghittu Landslide (BLs) has been identified (Fig. 11). To the southwest, the headwall is characterized by small tilted blocks sliding towards the deeper sector. The upper layers exhibit a wavy sliding pattern, also affecting the seabed (Fig. 11). Towards the northeast, the BLs becomes discontinuous and thins as it progresses deeper, eventually reaching the toe domain, where material accumulates. A basal detachment surface defines the sliding interface. Beneath the BLs, the older mass transport deposit MTD-CG2 is observed. In its headwall domain, parallel and continuous seismic layers are interrupted by a fault rooted in the bsz (Fig. 11b), which induces the chaotic rearrangement of the sediments. On the steeper sections (Fig. 11b), the body slides down, contributing to the accumulation at the slope base. Seismic imaging shows a deformation of the seabed concordant with the top of the chaotic layers of the MTD. It should also be noted the buried MTD recognized in the profile of Fig. 8 at a depth of about 1400 ms TWT. It reached a thickness of about 70 ms TWT, corresponding to 50–60 m. Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Marine and Petroleum Geology journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/marpetgeo Research paper Mass transport deposit (MTD) relief as a control on post-MTD sedimentation: Insights from the Taranaki Basin, oﬀshore New Zealand Jeﬀerson Nwoko∗, Ian Kane, Mads Huuse Basin Studies, School of Natural Sciences, University of Manchester, UK A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Mass transport deposits (MTDs) Turbidite Stratigraphic trap Seismic facies A B S T R A C T Mass transport deposits (MTDs) can aﬀect the routing of turbidite systems and their resultant sedimentary ar- chitecture. With the aid of a high-quality 3D-seismic reﬂection dataset a buried Pleistocene MTD has been identiﬁed in the deep-water Taranaki Basin. Three MTD seismic facies have been identiﬁed and fall within three morphological domains – extensional, translational and contractional areas. Thickness relationships between the MTD and the overburden deposits were investigated quantitatively and matched using TWT-thickness maps. The results reveal that in proximal and distal areas with signiﬁcant MTD relief, occupied by megaclasts and folded facies respectively, post-MTD accommodation is dominated by small ‘micro-basins’ with tortuous sediment pathways. In contrast the translational domain, containing homogeneous MTD facies, hosts larger low-relief basins. These characteristics can be used to deﬁne two main types of depositional topography: Type I, High-reli

is found within the sedimentary sequence. This unit is characterized by a transparent and chaotic seismic facies (Fig. 8). 4.2. Other recent mass movement systems On the northeast flank of Banghittu High, the here called Banghittu Landslide (BLs) has been identified (Fig. 11). To the southwest, the headwall is characterized by small tilted blocks sliding towards the deeper sector. The upper layers exhibit a wavy sliding pattern, also affecting the seabed (Fig. 11). Towards the northeast, the BLs becomes discontinuous and thins as it progresses deeper, eventually reaching the toe domain, where material accumulates. A basal detachment surface defines the sliding interface. Beneath the BLs, the older mass transport deposit MTD-CG2 is observed. In its headwall domain, parallel and continuous seismic layers are interrupted by a fault rooted in the bsz (Fig. 11b), which induces the chaotic rearrangement of the sediments. On the steeper sections (Fig. 11b), the body slides down, contributing to the accumulation at the slope base. Seismic imaging shows a deformation of the seabed concordant with the top of the chaotic layers of the MTD. It should also be noted the buried MTD recognized in the profile of Fig. 8 at a depth of about 1400 ms TWT. It reached a thickness of about 70 ms TWT, corresponding to 50–60 m. The MTD-CG1 has been inter- cepted by the profile on both sides of the Sarroch Canyon. Within the opaque wedge, an undeformed stratified block indicates les

is found within the sedimentary sequence. This unit is characterized by a transparent and chaotic seismic facies (Fig. 8). 4.2. To the southwest, the headwall is characterized by small tilted blocks sliding towards the deeper sector. The upper layers exhibit a wavy sliding pattern, also affecting the seabed (Fig. 11). Towards the northeast, the BLs becomes discontinuous and thins as it progresses deeper, eventually reaching the toe domain, where material accumulates. A basal detachment surface defines the sliding interface. Beneath the BLs, the older mass transport deposit MTD-CG2 is observed. In its headwall domain, parallel and continuous seismic layers are interrupted by a fault rooted in the bsz (Fig. 11b), which induces the chaotic rearrangement of the sediments. On the steeper sections (Fig. 11b), the body slides down, contributing to the accumulation at the slope base. Seismic imaging shows a deformation of the seabed concordant with the top of the chaotic layers of the MTD. It should also be noted the buried MTD recognized in the profile of Fig. 8 at a depth of about 1400 ms TWT. It reached a thickness of about 70 ms TWT, corresponding to 50–60 m. Seismic attributes Seismic attribute maps extracted from 3D seismic volume often reveal many notable characteristics that are not readily apparent from seismic cross − sections or temporal structural maps (Bull et al., 2009; Hart, 1999). To delineate MTD geometry and internal character, mul- tiple seismic attributes were extracted from the 3D volume. Coherence facilitated mapping discontinuities (faults, stratigraphic edges) and identifying chaotic facies associated with deformation (Deckers, 2015). Chaotic sections on coherence maps are seen as low coherence marked usually by dark, mottled patterns (Alves, 2015; Gamboa and Alves, 2015; Hart, 1999). Chaotic and disrupted seismic facies are easily discriminated from these maps (Bull et al., 2009). Seismic attribute of Root-Mean-Square (RMS) aided in recognizing the lateral boundary of sand and mud, leveraging the typically higher amplitude response of sands versus muds. (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022). Development of MTD depositional-type topography Slope stability is a function of the relationship between frictional and gravitational forces. Factors such as changes in slope loads, surface failure strength, slope gradient and nature of the basal shear surface can lead to instability (Sultan et al., 2004; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Li et al., 2015). These factors can facilitate MTD development and emplacement. Based on the seismic character, deformation style and structural features of folds, stratal disaggregation and faults, two main types of MTD depositional-type topography are developed during the emplacement of the youngest MTD in the Taranaki area. They have been identiﬁed as Type I: High-relief depositional topography and Type II: Low-relief depositional topography, controlled by MTD seismic fa- cies (Fig. 7a, b and 7c). Therefore, MTD depositional relief types can be eﬀectively linked to the MTD seismic facies (Table 1). Though both MTD type 1 and II topography types are generated by depositional processes, the results of this study shows that high surface irregularity often represents an overlap between structural internal deformation and depositional events that produces the MTD seismic facies. Seismic attributes Seismic attribute maps extracted from 3D seismic volume often reveal many notable characteristics that are not readily apparent from seismic cross − sections or temporal structural maps (Bull et al., 2009; Hart, 1999). To delineate MTD geometry and internal character, mul- tiple seismic attributes were extracted from the 3D volume. Coherence facilitated mapping discontinuities (faults, stratigraphic edges) and identifying chaotic facies associated with deformation (Deckers, 2015). Chaotic sections on coherence maps are seen as low coherence marked usually by dark, mottled patterns (Alves, 2015; Gamboa and Alves, 2015; Hart, 1999). Chaotic and disrupted seismic facies are easily discriminated from these maps (Bull et al., 2009). Seismic attribute of Root-Mean-Square (RMS) aided in recognizing the lateral boundary of sand and mud, leveraging the typically higher amplitude response of sands versus muds. (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022). Variance and dip attributes provided additional constraints on structural complexity and paleo-slope gradients within the deformed intervals, crucial for characterizing the MTD morphology and transport direction (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022; Banerjee et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2021). 3.4. Coherence facilitated mapping discontinuities (faults, stratigraphic edges) and identifying chaotic facies associated with deformation (Deckers, 2015). Chaotic sections on coherence maps are seen as low coherence marked usually by dark, mottled patterns (Alves, 2015; Gamboa and Alves, 2015; Hart, 1999). Chaotic and disrupted seismic facies are easily discriminated from these maps (Bull et al., 2009). Seismic attribute of Root-Mean-Square (RMS) aided in recognizing the lateral boundary of sand and mud, leveraging the typically higher amplitude response of sands versus muds. (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022). Variance and dip attributes provided additional constraints on structural complexity and paleo-slope gradients within the deformed intervals, crucial for characterizing the MTD morphology and transport direction (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022; Banerjee et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2021). 3.4. However, one is not entirely sure of the factors triggering slope failures in most documented cases of submarine slope instability. In particular, there is signiﬁcant less knowledge about the critical condi- tions under which a slope failure will occur. Most published data con- cerns ancient slope failures, with their formation mechanisms and triggers being hard to estimate. In addition, it is diﬃcult to predict when and where a slope failure will occur in the future, and there is an overall lack of in-situ measurements in the areas where recent sub- marine landslides have occurred. This study suggests that slope sediments can fail under their own gravity when gravitational forces along the slope overcome the shear strength of sediments. This failure mechanism does not require other triggers, such as earthquakes, gas hydrate dissolution and sea-level ﬂuctuations, and is likely responsible for some of the frequent sub- marine slope failures at the steep slopes, such as oﬀshore northern Norway where muddy contourites failed along the surfaces between them and coarse-grained turbidites on a steep continental slope (Laberg et al., 2016) (Fig. 11). Sediments are deformed along the transport, and the processes mainly include slides, slumps, and debris flow [14–16]. Concurrently, geotechnical measurements reveal an increase in shear strength, accompanied by a reduc- tion in void ratio and water content [17,18]. Sometimes, the related deposits are sand-prone and have good porosity and permeability as reservoirs [18–20]. _provenance.query: In what geological setting does mass transport deposit occur?
_provenance.context_preview:

youngest Mass Transport Deposit (MTD) located on the continental slope of the Gulf of Cagliari. By analysing the internal architecture and external morphology of the shallowest MTD in the region, called MTD-CG1, the study explores how pre-existing morphological features have influenced its evolution. A key research question is the relationship between the development of MTD-CG1 and the incision of the submarine canyon system in the Gulf of Cagliari. Specifically, this research examines the spatial distribution, relative age, and geological impact of MTD-CG1, investigating its interaction with the Sarroch, Sant’Elia-Foxy, and Carbonara canyon systems that cross the gulf. A fundamental question arises: does MTD-CG1 predate or postdate the formation of these canyons? Answering this requires determining whether the canyons incised an already deposited MTD- CG1 or, conversely, whether the mass transport deposit moved within pre-existing canyons. Understanding the influence of the canyon system on the deposition and movement of the MTD provides critical insights into the interplay between sedimentary transport, gravitational failure, and seafloor morphology. This research contributes to a deeper understanding of submarine slope instability and mass transport processes, offering valuable perspectives on the recent geological evolution of the region. 2. In addition, crescent-shaped bedforms, known as cyclic steps, have been identified within the head scarp of the Foxy Canyon (Deiana et al., 2017). These features, created by recurrent erosion and deposition driven by gravity, have wavelengths ranging from tens to a hundred meters and heights of several meters (Meleddu et al., 2016). In terms of sediment accumulation, high-energy events such as large-scale turbidity currents or slope subsidence are ruled out in the region. Instead, sedimentation is primarily attributed to low- intensity turbidity currents or hyperpycnal flows, which are consid- ered the main contributors to sediment transport and deposition in the basin (Paliaga, 2015). 3. Material and methods The main data used in this work were collected in the Gulf of Cagliari as part of the Western Sardinia project in a collaboration between the Istituto Nazionale di Oceanografia e di Geofisica Sperimentale (OGS) and the Universities of Trieste and Cagliari. _provenance.query: Does mass transport deposit occur in continental slope?
_provenance.context_preview:
[CHUNK 1 | unknown]
youngest Mass Transport Deposit (MTD) located on the continental slope of the Gulf of Cagliari. By analysing the internal architecture and external morphology of the shallowest MTD in the region, called MTD-CG1, the study explores how pre-existing morphological features have influenced its evolution. A key research question is the relationship between the development of MTD-CG1 and the incision of the submarine canyon system in the Gulf of Cagliari. Specifically, this research examines the spatial distribution, relative age, and geological impact of MTD-CG1, investigating its interaction with the Sarroch, Sant’Elia-Foxy, and Carbonara canyon systems that cross the gulf. A fundamental question arises: does MTD-CG1 predate or postdate the formation of these canyons? Answering this requires determining whether the canyons incised an already deposited MTD- CG1 or, conversely, whether the mass transport deposit moved within pre-existing canyons. Understanding the influence of the canyon system on the deposition and movement of the MTD provides critical insights into the interplay between sedimentary transport, gravitational failure, and seafloor morphology. This research contributes to a deeper understanding of submarine slope instability and mass transport processes, offering valuable perspectives on the recent geological evolution of the region. 2. In MTD4, reworked facies are slightly finer and muddier than the under- lying deposits and the folded beds show a partial lateral continuity, despite the presence of sheath folds. Keywords: mass transport deposits; controlling factors; correlation analysis; recursive feature elimination; random forest artificial intelligence algorithm; Pliocene Qiongdongnan Basin 1. Introduction Mass transport deposits (MTDs) widely occur worldwide [1–5] and are unstable transport caused by various controlling factors (high sedimentation rates, paleo–marine landslides and mass failures, sea level fluctuations, volcanism, water temperatures, ocean currents, tsunamis, storms, hurricanes, tides, earthquakes, gas-hydrate dissociation, glacia- tion, etc.) on deep-water, shelf-margins or slopes, canyons, uplifted tectonic flanks, and channel sidewalls [6–13]. Sediments are deformed along the transport, and the processes mainly include slides, slumps, and debris flow [14–16]. The log responses typically demon- strate elevated resistivity, compressional velocity, density, and porosity. Concurrently, geotechnical measurements reveal an increase in shear strength, accompanied by a reduc- tion in void ratio and water content [17,18]. Sometimes, the related deposits are sand-prone and have good porosity and permeability as reservoirs [18–20]. MTDs could be one of the major natural hazards in deep-water areas of both lacustrine and ocean settings. When the J. Mar. Sci. The prevailing understanding characterizes MTDs as gravity-driven instability phenomena occurring on the seafloors. This study employs high- resolution 2D/3D seismic data integrated with well data to re-evaluate the genesis mechanisms of MTDs in the Kaiping sag, Zhu-II depression, Pearl River Mouth basin (PRMB), northern margin of the South China Sea (SCS). Key diagnostic features interpreted from seismic datasets reveal the absence of typical characteristics of con- ventional MTDs, including no distinct headwall scarps, and moats, and especially no onlapping structures of isochronous turbidite contourites that usually cover uneven MTD surfaces. Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Marine and Petroleum Geology journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/marpetgeo Research paper Mass transport deposit (MTD) relief as a control on post-MTD sedimentation: Insights from the Taranaki Basin, oﬀshore New Zealand Jeﬀerson Nwoko∗, Ian Kane, Mads Huuse Basin Studies, School of Natural Sciences, University of Manchester, UK A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Mass transport deposits (MTDs) Turbidite Stratigraphic trap Seismic facies A B S T R A C T Mass transport deposits (MTDs) can aﬀect the routing of turbidite systems and their resultant sedimentary ar- chitecture. With the aid of a high-quality 3D-seismic reﬂection dataset a buried Pleistocene MTD has been identiﬁed in the deep-water Taranaki Basin. By analysing the internal architecture and external morphology of the shallowest MTD in the region, called MTD-CG1, the study explores how pre-existing morphological features have influenced its evolution. A key research question is the relationship between the development of MTD-CG1 and the incision of the submarine canyon system in the Gulf of Cagliari. Specifically, this research examines the spatial distribution, relative age, and geological impact of MTD-CG1, investigating its interaction with the Sarroch, Sant’Elia-Foxy, and Carbonara canyon systems that cross the gulf. A fundamental question arises: does MTD-CG1 predate or postdate the formation of these canyons? Answering this requires determining whether the canyons incised an already deposited MTD- CG1 or, conversely, whether the mass transport deposit moved within pre-existing canyons. Understanding the influence of the canyon system on the deposition and movement of the MTD provides critical insights into the interplay between sedimentary transport, gravitational failure, and seafloor morphology. This research contributes to a deeper understanding of submarine slope instability and mass transport processes, offering valuable perspectives on the recent geological evolution of the region. 2. The seismic criteria used to distinguish seismogenic defor- mation from other mechanisms (e.g., gravity-driven sliding) include: (1) abrupt, discrete deformation zones: MTD intervals contain narrow, sharply bounded zones of chaotic reflectors, lacking the gradual, wide- spread strain associated with protracted deformation (e.g., Fig. 6c shows a confined shear zone beneath the allochthonous wedge, not distributed layer-parallel slippage); (2) syn-deformational stratigraphic relation- ships: Deformed units are immediately overlain by conformable, un- disturbed strata (e.g., the allochthonous wedge at mge-1 is capped by continuous T35–T32 reflections), indicating rapid, episodic emplace- ment rather than slow, incremental deformation; (3) association with thrust fault reactivation: Each inferred mega-earthquake coincides with large (~20 ms in TWT) thrust offsets (e.g., mge-1 aligns with a thrust zone active at ~ 13.8 Ma, Fig. 6c), consistent with tectonic earthquakes correlating with fault slip in rifted margins (Yan et al., 2008; Luan et al., 2024). 5. According to Shanmugam’s (2020) recent classification, MTDs consist of three basic types: (1) slides, characterized by coherent translation without rotation; (2) slumps, involving coherent translation with rotation; and (3) debris flow. Turbidite sandstone distribution aﬀected by Type I topography, is characterised by irregular pinch-outs which present a signiﬁcant risk for stratigraphic trapping whereas Type II topography may form overlying sedimentary bodies which are more extensive. Turbidite deposits in both settings are prone to syn-depositional remobilisation on top of the MTD, which may introduce considerable heterogeneity, particularly at reservoir margins. 1. Introduction Mass Transport Deposits (MTDs) are sedimentary bodies that are translated downslope from topographic highs and slopes to areas of lower gradient as a result of gravity–induced mass failure (Hampton et al., 1996; Moscardelli and Wood, 2008; Kneller et al., 2016; Nwoko et al., 2020). They commonly contain sediments remobilised from re- latively shallower water depth which have been redeposited on the slopes and ﬂoor of deep water basins (Posamentier and Walker, 2006; Kneller et al., 2016). MTDs may cover large areas (kilometres across) and are therefore very important to exploration and production activ- ities in many hydrocarbon provinces; acting as seals or preconditioning reservoir geometries (Armitage et al., 2009; Omeru, 2014). The chaotic facies suggest debris or turbidity flow activity, characterized by rafted blocks suspended within the flows (Fig. 9). A critical distinction from conventional MTDs lies in the absence of the characteristic uneven surface typically associated with overlying turbidite contourites (Kiswaka et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2024) (Fig. 9). We interpret this feature as indicative of in-situ liquefaction within a confined space (where strata were already overlain by overburden), rather than a submarine slide occurring on an open seafloor. Along the downslope direction (Figs. 9d, e), the base of the chaotic facies exhibits a rugged texture, contrasting with the typical smooth, concave-upward glide planes over which the MDTs usually move. Across the slope, the base of the chaotic facies displays a morphology (Fig. 8) resembling river channels; however, these channels are filled with chaotic facies rather than turbidite contourites, and their density is anomalously high (10 river channels per 25 km in this study vs 1 channel per 25 km in South China Sea slope context, Yang et al., 2024). The low slope gradient (~0.3–0.6◦) in the Kaiping sag directly contradicts the prevailing understanding that MTDs form via gravity-driven sliding on steep slopes (Shanmugam, 2012, 2020, 2021). Instead, our study proposes a hypothesis that dynamic triggers, such as mega-earthquakes, induce the chaotic facies and the subsequent limited material migration. Mega-earthquakes generate dynamic shocks that liquefy unconsolidated sediments, enabling chaotic facies genera- tion regardless of whether slopes are gentle or steep. Gravity-driven mechanisms rely primarily on slope angle, while our hypothesis high- light the crucial role of earthquake shock in the formation of the chaotic facies within confined environments. Introduction Mass transport deposits (MTDs) widely occur worldwide [1–5] and are unstable transport caused by various controlling factors (high sedimentation rates, paleo–marine landslides and mass failures, sea level fluctuations, volcanism, water temperatures, ocean currents, tsunamis, storms, hurricanes, tides, earthquakes, gas-hydrate dissociation, glacia- tion, etc.) on deep-water, shelf-margins or slopes, canyons, uplifted tectonic flanks, and channel sidewalls [6–13]. Sediments are deformed along the transport, and the processes mainly include slides, slumps, and debris flow [14–16]. The log responses typically demon- strate elevated resistivity, compressional velocity, density, and porosity. Concurrently, geotechnical measurements reveal an increase in shear strength, accompanied by a reduc- tion in void ratio and water content [17,18]. Sometimes, the related deposits are sand-prone and have good porosity and permeability as reservoirs [18–20]. MTDs could be one of the major natural hazards in deep-water areas of both lacustrine and ocean settings. When the J. Mar. Sci. MF description: This facies is well imaged and covers an area of approximately 413.82 km2. From northeast to southwest, a dominantly moderate to high amplitude reﬂection pattern that typically delineates angular protruding megaclasts is observed (Fig. 4b and c). However, one is not entirely sure of the factors triggering slope failures in most documented cases of submarine slope instability. In particular, there is signiﬁcant less knowledge about the critical condi- tions under which a slope failure will occur. Most published data con- cerns ancient slope failures, with their formation mechanisms and triggers being hard to estimate. In addition, it is diﬃcult to predict when and where a slope failure will occur in the future, and there is an overall lack of in-situ measurements in the areas where recent sub- marine landslides have occurred. This study suggests that slope sediments can fail under their own gravity when gravitational forces along the slope overcome the shear strength of sediments.
